summary.brief: Kevin Clanton v. United States. Medical malpractice case involving oncology.

evidence_index.text: Clanton alleged that nurse practitioner Denise Jordan, an employee of the U.S. Public Health Service, failed to educate him about his severe hypertension or to monitor its advancement, and as a result of that negligent care his hypertension developed into Stage V kidney disease.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Clanton required dialysis and, at the age of 35, a kidney transplant, and is expected to endure further cycles of dialysis and another transplant in the future.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Following a five-day bench trial, the district court found the United States liable, rejected the government’s comparative-negligence argument as to Clanton, and awarded Clanton nearly $30 million in damages.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: That included testimony from three expert witnesses, establishing: that patients often feel well when they have high blood pressure, which provides 'misinformation' to them as to whether they are ill; that it is common for people to think that if they do not feel sick there is no need for treatment; that high blood pressure is a silent killer, and that patient education is absolutely essential to controlling it especially in a young person who is facing something that does not produce any symptoms which would let him know what is happening.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witnesses

evidence_index.text: Clanton began undergoing hemodialysis in March 2013 and received a kidney transplant in November 2015.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The district court held that under that reasonable-person standard, Clanton was not comparatively negligent.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The court considered not only what Clanton knew or would conclude as a result of his medical interactions, but what a reasonable person would conclude.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: During Clanton’s final visit in October 2012, Jordan again ordered lab work, which revealed extensive kidney damage.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The decision of the district court is AFFIRMED.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: High blood pressure noted during pre-employment physical
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient was informed of high blood pressure during a pre-employment physical in June 2008 and sought care at Quick Care Clinic.

requestables.name: Routine lab work
requestables.description: Lab work ordered to assess general health status

requestables.name: Kidney function tests
requestables.description: Lab tests to assess kidney function

reveal.result_summary: Signs of kidney disease in 2011, extensive kidney damage in 2012
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicated progression to chronic kidney disease
reveal.decision_impact: Should have prompted referral to nephrologist

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with high blood pressure during pre-employment physical

timeline_phases.name: Follow-up and Monitoring
timeline_phases.description: Patient returns for follow-up visits over several years

key_events.event: Lab tests ordered revealing kidney disease
Extensive kidney damage revealed in lab tests

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with hypertension and lab results indicating kidney disease
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management after receiving lab results indicating kidney disease?

options.description: Refer to nephrologist for further evaluation
options.clinical_reasoning: Referral to a specialist is standard when lab results indicate kidney disease

options.description: Continue current management without specialist referral
options.clinical_reasoning: Failed to address the progression of kidney disease

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not refer to nephrologist despite lab results

expected_action_court.description: Referral to nephrologist should have been made
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated referral was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to act on lab results indicating kidney disease
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Referral to nephrologist for specialized care
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed Stage V kidney disease requiring dialysis and kidney transplant
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Chronic kidney disease requiring ongoing treatment

key_findings: Failure to educate patient about hypertension and its risks
Failure to act on lab results indicating kidney disease

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to refer to nephrologist and educate patient
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Referral and education could have prevented progression to Stage V kidney disease

factual_timeline.event: Patient informed of high blood pressure during pre-employment physical
Lab tests ordered revealing signs of kidney disease
Lab tests revealed extensive kidney damage

allegations.allegation: Failure to educate patient about hypertension
Failure to act on lab results indicating kidney disease

court_findings.legal_significance: Failure to educate and refer patient
